evidence: Top-level packages (web, shared, cli) map to clear domain capabilities: web = Next.js frontend + API, shared = cross-package types, cli = developer tooling
Auth is split across four files (auth.ts, admin-auth.ts, agent-auth.ts, internal-auth.ts) each with clear, documented responsibility boundaries and distinct auth flows

ConnectionMethod type is defined three times independently: packages/shared/types/agent.ts (ConnectionMethod), packages/web/lib/agent-factory.ts (VALID_CONNECTION_METHODS + ConnectionMethodValue), and packages/web/components/modals/agent/types.ts (ConnectionMethodType) — all with identical values but no shared source of truth
API route structure cleanly separates public (api/v1/), user-facing (api/servers/, api/dms/), and internal (api/internal/) surfaces with predictable URL patterns
components/ directory is organized by domain: chat/, dm/, layout/, workspace/, modals/, providers/, server-settings/, user/ — each directory maps to a clear UI capability

DM chat area (dm/dm-chat-area.tsx, 428 lines) is a standalone monolith that re-imports shared chat components (MessageInput, MarkdownContent, ReactionBar, EditMessageInput) piecemeal rather than sharing a common message rendering abstraction with chat/chat-area.tsx
shared/types/ barrel export (index.ts) re-exports all domain types cleanly: user, server, channel, message, agent, protocol, typed-messages — each file maps to one Prisma model or protocol surface
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Triple definition of ConnectionMethod type across shared types, agent-factory, and modal types creates a maintenance hazard where the three definitions can drift independently. DM chat area duplicates rendering logic that should be shared with channel chat area through a common abstraction.

strengths: Auth responsibility is decomposed into four focused files (auth.ts, admin-auth.ts, agent-auth.ts, internal-auth.ts) each handling a single auth flow with documented security properties
API route tree cleanly separates three audiences (public v1, user-facing, internal service-to-service) with consistent URL patterns
shared/types/ provides a single canonical source for domain models that mirrors the Prisma schema, with each file owning one bounded context
components/ directory structure maps predictably to UI capabilities — a new developer can locate code by feature name
Protocol types in shared/types/protocol.ts exactly mirror docs/PROTOCOL.md, making the documentation trustworthy as a source of truth
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in type ownership fragmentation — the same domain concept (ConnectionMethod) is defined independently in three locations without a canonical source, and the DM chat surface duplicates rendering patterns rather than sharing them with the primary chat surface.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase has clear, domain-aligned package decomposition and predictable ownership boundaries. A developer can explain why each top-level directory exists and where to find or place new code. The auth file split is exemplary — each file has a single, documented responsibility. However, the triple ConnectionMethod definition is a genuine decomposition failure that creates maintenance risk and signals incomplete type consolidation. The DM/channel chat duplication further suggests the decomposition doesn't fully localize change for cross-cutting message rendering concerns. These issues prevent the score from reaching 'strong' (90) territory but the overall structure is solid.

issues.summary: ConnectionMethod type defined independently in three locations with no shared canonical source
evidence: packages/shared/types/agent.ts line 6 defines ConnectionMethod as a union type
packages/web/lib/agent-factory.ts lines 14-23 defines VALID_CONNECTION_METHODS array and ConnectionMethodValue type with the same values
packages/web/components/modals/agent/types.ts line 6 defines ConnectionMethodType as a third identical union type
issues.suggestion: Consolidate to a single definition in packages/shared/types/agent.ts. Export both the type (ConnectionMethod) and a runtime validation array (VALID_CONNECTION_METHODS) from there. Have agent-factory.ts and modal types.ts import from shared instead of redeclaring.

issues.summary: DM chat area duplicates message rendering logic instead of sharing with channel chat area
evidence: dm-chat-area.tsx (428 lines) imports MessageInput, MarkdownContent, EditMessageInput, ReactionBar, DeleteMessageModal individually from chat/ — the same components used by chat-area.tsx
dm-chat-area.tsx reimplements message list rendering, scroll management, and edit/delete state management that chat-area.tsx delegates to MessageList
The dm/ directory contains only dm-chat-area.tsx, suggesting it is a parallel implementation rather than a distinct domain module
issues.suggestion: Extract a shared MessageList-like component that both ChatArea and DmChatArea use, or make ChatArea accept a mode prop that disables streaming/agent features for DM contexts. This localizes message rendering changes to one place.

issues.summary: agent-auth.ts contains two near-identical functions with duplicated Prisma query and result mapping
evidence: authenticateAgentRequest (lines 27-68) and authenticateAgentKey (lines 74-113) both perform identical Prisma queries (findFirst with same select clause) and identical result mapping to AgentAuthResult
The only difference is input source: one reads from request headers, the other takes a raw string. The core lookup logic is duplicated verbatim across ~35 lines
issues.suggestion: Extract the shared lookup into a private helper like lookupAgentByKeyHash(apiKeyHash: string): Promise<AgentAuthResult | null>, then have both public functions call it after their respective input parsing.

root_causes: ConnectionMethod type fragmentation likely arose from independent feature development (shared types, server-side factory, UI modal) without enforcing a single canonical source
DM chat area was likely built as a simplified copy of channel chat area to avoid coupling, but the duplication grew as shared concerns (reactions, editing, deletion) were added to both
